course_description: Analysis of the macroeconomy as a complex system. How macroeconomic factors & domestic macroeconomic policies affect the business conditions faced by large & small firms. Introduction to macroeconomic modelling, forecasting & scenario planning.

requirements.assumed_background: Although it is an advantage to have studied ECON1020 (Introductory Macroeconomics) compared to ECON1011 (Economics for Business), competent students without this more intensive background should be able to do well this course. The three macroeconomics topics in ECON1011 provide a good, accelerated introduction to many core concepts, and revision by accessing the learning resources on the ECON1011 site from Semester 1 2020 is recommended. A solid background means, for example, that you know that the technical meaning of several terms (such as "saving" and "investment") differ from their conventional, every-day use outside macroeconomics. Familiarity with simple diagrams in economics, especially S&D or AS&AD (shifts of lines vs movements along) is very helpful. If your background is not strong, do keep up each week, study the Course Notes, and use the consultation sessions.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Tutorials will commence in Week 2 . Students are required to select their preferred class times through My Timetable and attend their allocated timeslot.

Refer to the timetable prior to the commencement of classes for the most up to date information, as late room changes may sometimes occur. The timetable can be downloaded here Public Timetable .

Please see the Learning Activities section of this Course Profile (Section 4.1) for the timetabling implications of public holidays.

Public Holidays: Fri. 14 August, Mon. 5 October (Queen's Birthday).

Mid-Semester Break: 28 September - 5 October. Classes will resume on 6 October.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
The aims of this course are for students to:

Read business conditions better.

Identify opportunities and dangers in diverse short-term and long-term business conditions.

Cope better with uncertainties in the business environment.

Use macroeconomic theory to organise thinking about causes and effects.

Avoid errors commonly made by those without good training in macroeconomics.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify typical co-movements in macroeconomic data (e.g. GDP, inflation, interest rates, fiscal position, balance of trade).
Distinguish between causal explanations relevant to short-term conditions and long-term conditions.
Understand an economy as an interconnected system.
Recognise that system dynamics are sometimes stable and sometimes unstable depending on the type and size of the shock, the point of its impact, and the structure and initial state of the system.
Extract and interpret key information from the main publications of Australia's Commonwealth Treasury and Reserve Bank.
Write succinct analyses of the effects of macroeconomic changes on at least one specific industry.

assessment_details.title: Using the right model well
assessment_details.task_description:
This is a collection of 8-12 exercises, each worth between 3 and 8 marks. They form one assignment worth 50 marks in total. Answer and upload in one document. Most of the exercises can be answered in about 100 well-chosen words per exercise. Allow up to a page per exercise, especially if diagrams are required or are included. Further details will be posted on Blackboard.

Some exercises will require application of macroeconomic reasoning to a given scenario that you perhaps have not encountered before. They may refer to events that occur during the semester. Study of the Course Notes, Lectures, and Tutorials is the way to prepare, but good answers cannot easily be constructed from recycling answers already available online. Some exercises aim to reveal how well you can analyse problems, or make decisions, under conditions of macroeconomic uncertainty.

It is important to choose the right 'model'/theory according to the relevant time period (i.e. short term vs long term). It is a common pitfall to pick a theory that does not apply to the relevant time frame.

Some exercises may relate to identifying the range of effects of events on a particular industry, and you may need to do some quick research on reputable websites.

or

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Upload via course website, using Turnitin.

assessment_details.title: Policy review and analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
Report on ONE of the following two options:

i) policy challenges and options identified by the Australian central bank (the RBA) in the light of COVID-19.

ii) policy challenges and options identified by the Australian Commonwealth Treasury in the light of COVID-19.

In your report, also consider the impact of these policy responses on business conditions in one Australian industry. (Aim for between 100 and 400 words.) This industry may be the one in which you plan or hope to find a future career or find business opportunities. If you are an overseas student, imagine that you are located in Australia.

It is allowable to consider likely interconnections between fiscal and monetary policy, if appropriate. It is allowable to refer to how Australian policy options or debates may differ from those in another country.

Total length: 1700-2100 words, excluding references and citations.

Total: 50 marks.

Refer to Blackboard for some guidance on a) effective research and report writing and b) meeting the assessment criteria.

Note that feedback throughout the course and before you submit assessment is available by a) optional online quizzes on basic macroeconomic concepts and reasoning (no marks are attached), b) tutorials and c) consultation sessions.

You may email the Course Coordinator an early draft or an advanced draft of your report to get feedback on whether you are on the right track or writing in a suitable way. However, the quantity and quality of the feedback may be affected by class size and the percentage of students who seek feedback, so don't leave things too late.

All courses are supported by the UQ Learning Management System, Blackboard Learn, which provides an interactive environment for the students and teachers. This is accessible via PC or Mac or Mobile (Apps) environment. To log on to Blackboard, go to https://learn.uq.edu.au .

Or help with Learn.UQ (Blackboard):

see https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library‐ services/it/learnuq‐blackboard‐help/learnuq‐blackboard‐contacts

contact AskUs https://web.library.uq.edu.au/contact‐us

or telephone 334 64312.

Lecture Recordings

The University supports the recording of teaching, for example lectures, to supplement face‐to‐face teaching. This policy reflects the University’s commitment to students taking responsibility for their own learning, as well as to flexible provision of access to supplementary learning resources. Owing to COVID restrictions, lecture attendance may be impossible, so recordings are a substitute.

Slides from the lecture/live class will be placed on Blackboard. (Some slides and animations are not supplied but can be screen captured.) You are expected to bring lecture slides (printed or on a device) when viewing lectures. The contents of the lectures/ live classes do not constitute the entire assessable course content. Assessment is online and cannot be based merely on copying lecture material, answers to tutorial questions, or extracts from course notes. Course materials and classes provide the foundations both for your independent research report and for the tests of your ability to use the right theory and valid reasoning to problems you may not have entirely seen before.

Note

These lecture/live-class slides are a useful learning guide but they are not a substitute for any set of essential readings or exercises specified in the reading list on Blackboard or in the course profile.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Thu 14:00 L1 The 'Macroeconomy' & 'Business Conditions' : The basic concepts of macroeconomics: 'the economy': comparing micro and macro behaviour. Interconnections and structure, not just adding up output or averaging prices.

Mon - Fri Tutorials start in week 2. : Tutorials typically focus on aspects of the previous week's lectures, but some may give pointers to upcoming assignments. Course Notes are provided for each topic.

Thu L2 Trends and fluctuations : Basic patterns and key terminology. Normal, stable dynamics: auto-correction. Abnormal dynamics: amplification and contagion. Crises. Inertia and tipping points. Hysteresis.

Mon - Fri T1: Reviewing basic macro : Friday 14 August is the Exhibition Day Public Holiday, with no classes. See Blackboard for alternative tutorial arrangements. Tutorial on parts of the work covered in L1 and Topic Notes.

Thu L3 Equilibrium in output markets : Standard models of equilibrium in well-behaved systems: AS-AD (with inflation on the vertical axis vs P). Standard trajectories for demand shocks and supply and price (cost-side) shocks. C+I+G+EXP-IMP briefly revisited.
Mon - Fri T2 More reviewing basic Macro from L1 & L2 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.

Thu L4 Fiscal & monetary policy : Inflation targeting and emergency actions; in theory and in Australia's case. Budget policy and emergency actions; in theory and in Australia's case. Avoiding common errors. Introduction to Assignment One.
Mon - Fri T3 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture 3 and Topic Notes.

Thu L5 Differences between markets : Price expectations. Sales expectations. Asset markets and expectations, including bond markets. Stocks dominate flows.'Black Swans' & 'fat tails'. Goods and services and expectations. Labour market(s) and expectations, a large and unique 'market'.
Mon - Fri T4 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.
Thu L6 Combining goods and financial markets : IS-LM made simpler and more useful. Monetary policy during fluctuations. Fiscal policy during fluctuations.
Mon - Fri T5 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.
Thu L7 Short-term forecasting : Combining data and theories of causation. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 5 Readings/Ref: CN_BL
Mon - Fri T6 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.

Thu L8 The reform agenda and international factors : Why institutions and norms matter. Tax reform? Implications of openness in goods and financial markets, and the goods market in an open economy. More neoliberalism or more nationalism? A false choice?
Mon - Fri T7 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.
Mon - Sun Mid Semester Break : No lecture
Mon - Sun Mid Semester Break : No tutorials
Thu L9 The business climate: technology and saving : Technological progress and growth. Secular stagnation? The contested role of saving rates.

Mon - Fri T8 : Queen's Birthday Public Holiday - Monday 5th. No classes on Monday. Check Blackboard for alternative tutorial arrangements. Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.

Thu L10 Longer-term forecasting and scenario planning : Interpreting macro data; avoiding common errors. Applying macro-theory to assess business conditions. Making crucial decisions under acute uncertainty.
Mon - Fri T9 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes.
Thu L11 Wrapping up &/or catching up : Note that Assignment 2 is due on Friday 23 October. Readings/Ref: CN_BL
Mon - Fri T10 : Tutorial on parts of the work covered in previous week's Lecture and Topic Notes. Last-minute guidance on Assignment 2.
Thu Marking Week: no lecture
Mon - Fri No classes : No tutorials this week: assignments are already submitted.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

Viewing sessions and/or feedback opportunities will be available, subject in form to COVID19 restrictions. Further details will be posted on blackboard at the relevant time.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing, please refer to the guides available in https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/referencing?b=g&d=a&group_id=15017 .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The Course has a new Course Coordinator and is redesigned. Furthermore, COVID-19 requires changes to assessment. This year there is no final exam. There are two online assignments only.

The main aim of the course is to enable students to understand how macroeconomic factors and domestic macroeconomic policies affect the business conditions faced by large and small firms. It emphasises the application of macroeconomic analysis. The main focus is on mature economies like Australia.

Economic conditions affect business decisions. Three major determinants of macroeconomic conditions are GDP, inflation and interest rates.

The business environment can change suddenly due to diverse shocks on the supply and demand sides. Some disturbances are familiar, and their consequences follow predictable patterns. Businesses need to interpret events quickly and act accordingly. However, some are novel in type or size (e.g. the financial crisis of 2007-2008 and its aftermath, or the COVID-19 outbreak) and decisions need to be made in conditions of widespread uncertainty and stress. Sometimes shocks have irresistible force and businesses need to become resilient and adaptable.

Governments use fiscal and monetary policy, often in systematic ways. Understanding the rationale behind these policies means being taken by surprise less often.

Institutions and laws affect the overall environment within which longer-term decisions are made. Making overseas investments requires assessing economic, political and social conditions in foreign countries.

Economies evolve and transform gradually over the decades. They can also move suddenly between boom and slump. Decision-makers need to assess market conditions and anticipate what may happen.

What are some typical patterns to recognise? How and why do disturbances in some parts of the economy spread to others? The course considers the crucial roles of financial and asset markets.

Private-sector investment affects business conditions both during short-term fluctuations in GDP and over the trend. Thus business decisions themselves shape business conditions.

This course is incompatible with ECON2020, which means, for example, that if you are enrolled in the BEcon, this course will not earn you any credit towards your degree under the current rules.

This course is not equivalent to ECON2020 and it may not be used as a formal pre-requisite for further studies in macroeconomics.

This course is designed for students not enrolled in an Economics degree program but who want simplified accounts of some of the highlights of other courses in Economics that may be of practical use or of wider interest.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The course has been redesigned in 2020, and there is a new course coordinator. I look forward to your feedback both informally during the semester and formally in the surveys.

B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .

C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .

D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Note!

This course has no textbook, so pay close attention to announcements on the course website, the lectures and Course Notes. There are many pitfalls in macroeconomics, and even textbooks can make errors or frame things in a misleading way. E.g. a textbook diagram may be drawn 'correctly', given its assumptions. However, these assumptions (often unstated) may be unrealistic or inappropriate in important practical situations.

For Assignment 1 (Report), parts of the lectures, tutorials and Course (Topic) Notes are directly relevant. However, extra research will be needed, especially using the websites of government agencies and other reputable sites.

It is extremely important that you do not rely on 'internet research' for Assignment Two (Exercises). Googling the answer is likely to result in a catastrophic failing score.

Enquiries

All administrative enquiries (e.g. allocation to tutorials, records of progressive assessment) should be directed to the Course Administrator, Marie Taylor.

All enquiries relating to the contents of the course materials, activities and assessment standards should be directed to the Course Coordinator, Bruce Littleboy.

Consultation

COVID-19 restrictions will limit face-to-face, live consultation. Teaching staff have set consultation times during which you may 'see' them without making an appointment. These are posted on the course Blackboard site. Consultation outside these times is by appointment with the Course Coordinator only. Where suitable, you may also use the course’s online Discussion Board to seek assistance from your peers.

Do not expect tutors to coach you through the two assignments. They can offer general guidance only (e.g. warning you that you are on the wrong track) and they can at most simply point you towards the relevant sources or to parts of the lectures or topic notes.

late_submission:
If you have a valid reason (as defined by the University regulation) for not taking completing assessment tasks by the due date and time, you should inform the Course Coordinator as early as possible, preferably before the due date and a specific arrangement will be made on a case-by-case basis.

Requests for the granting of extensions must be made online via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email.

Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment may be accessed through: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/extension-progressive-assessment .

COVID-19 temporary changes to assessment extension procedures : If you are requesting an extension to assessment based on medical grounds, we do not require a medical certificate. We will accept a statement of circumstances signed by you until 1 November 2020 .
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other

The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

SUBMISSION OF ASSIGNMENTS

All assignments must be submitted by the due date and time stated in the course profile. For this course, students are required to submit assignments electronically via Turnitin on the course Blackboard site.
